title: SQL Aggregations & GROUP BY
description: Aggregate functions, grouping data, HAVING clause, and advanced grouping operations.

questions.text: Which aggregate function counts the total number of rows in a result set?

options.text: SUM()
TOTAL()
COUNT()
NUMBER()

questions.text: What is the difference between COUNT(*) and COUNT(ColumnName)?

options.text: COUNT(*) counts all rows including NULLs; COUNT(ColumnName) counts only non-NULL values in that column
COUNT(*) is slower; COUNT(ColumnName) is faster
They are identical
COUNT(*) counts distinct values; COUNT(ColumnName) counts all values

questions.text: Which function returns the total sum of a numeric column?

options.text: TOTAL()
ADD()
SUM()
ACCUMULATE()

questions.text: What does AVG(Salary) return?

options.text: The median salary
The arithmetic average of non-NULL Salary values
The sum of all salaries
The most common salary value

questions.text: Which query finds the highest salary in the Employees table?

options.text: SELECT HIGHEST(Salary) FROM Employees
SELECT TOP(Salary) FROM Employees
SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM Employees
SELECT GREATEST(Salary) FROM Employees

questions.text: Which query counts the number of employees in each department?

options.text: SELECT Department, COUNT(*) FROM Employees WHERE Department
SELECT Department, COUNT(*) FROM Employees GROUP BY Department
SELECT COUNT(*) BY Department FROM Employees
SELECT COUNT(Department) FROM Employees

questions.text: When using GROUP BY with multiple columns, what does it group by?

options.text: Only the first column listed
Each unique combination of values across all listed columns
All columns independently
It is invalid to group by more than one column

questions.text: What is the purpose of the HAVING clause?

options.text: To filter individual rows before grouping, similar to WHERE
To filter groups after aggregation has been applied
To define which columns appear in the GROUP BY
To replace the WHERE clause when using joins

questions.text: Which query finds departments where the average salary exceeds 60000?

options.text: SELECT Department FROM Employees WHERE AVG(Salary) > 60000
SELECT Department, AVG(Salary) FROM Employees GROUP BY Department HAVING AVG(Salary) > 60000
SELECT Department FROM Employees GROUP BY Department WHERE AVG(Salary) > 60000
SELECT Department FROM Employees HAVING AVG(Salary) > 60000

questions.text: What is the key difference between WHERE and HAVING?

options.text: WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after aggregation
HAVING filters rows before grouping; WHERE filters after
WHERE can use aggregate functions; HAVING cannot
They are interchangeable when using GROUP BY

questions.text: How does GROUP BY treat NULL values?

options.text: NULL values are ignored and excluded from grouping
NULLs in the GROUP BY column are grouped together into a single NULL group
An error is raised if any GROUP BY column contains NULL
Each NULL is treated as a separate unique group

questions.text: What does ROLLUP do in a GROUP BY clause?

options.text: Returns all possible combinations of grouping columns
Generates subtotals and grand totals along a hierarchy of grouping columns
Sorts the result by the aggregated values
Filters out NULL groups

questions.text: What is the difference between ROLLUP and CUBE?

options.text: ROLLUP generates hierarchical subtotals; CUBE generates all possible combinations of subtotals
CUBE generates hierarchical subtotals; ROLLUP generates all combinations
They are identical
ROLLUP only works with two columns; CUBE works with any number

questions.text: What does GROUPING SETS allow you to do?

options.text: Group by all columns simultaneously
Specify multiple individual grouping combinations in a single query
Create a new table from group results
Apply different aggregate functions to each group

questions.text: What does SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT Department) FROM Employees return?

options.text: The total number of employees
The number of employees per department
The number of unique department values
An error — DISTINCT cannot be used inside COUNT

questions.text: Which of the following correctly uses SUM with a JOIN to get total order amounts per customer?

options.text: SELECT c.Name, SUM(o.Amount) FROM Customers c JOIN Orders o ON c.ID = o.CustomerID GROUP BY c.Name
SELECT c.Name, SUM(o.Amount) FROM Customers c JOIN Orders o ON c.ID = o.CustomerID
SELECT c.Name, SUM(o.Amount) FROM Customers c JOIN Orders o GROUP BY SUM(o.Amount)
SELECT SUM(o.Amount) GROUP BY c.Name FROM Customers c JOIN Orders o

questions.text: What is wrong with this query? SELECT Department, Name, COUNT(*) FROM Employees GROUP BY Department

options.text: COUNT(*) cannot be used with GROUP BY
'Name' is not in the GROUP BY clause and is not inside an aggregate function, making it invalid
Nothing is wrong; it will work correctly
You cannot SELECT the grouped column in SQL

questions.text: Does AVG() include NULL values in its calculation?

options.text: Yes, NULLs are treated as 0 in AVG()
No, AVG() ignores NULL values
Yes, NULLs cause AVG() to return NULL
It depends on the database

questions.text: What does MIN() return when applied to a text column?

options.text: An error — MIN() only works on numbers
The shortest string
The alphabetically first value
NULL always

questions.text: Which query is correct for finding customers with more than 5 orders?

options.text: SELECT CustomerID, COUNT(*) AS OrderCount FROM Orders WHERE COUNT(*) > 5 GROUP BY CustomerID
SELECT CustomerID, COUNT(*) AS OrderCount FROM Orders GROUP BY CustomerID HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
SELECT CustomerID FROM Orders GROUP BY CustomerID WHERE OrderCount > 5
SELECT CustomerID FROM Orders HAVING COUNT(*) > 5

questions.text: Can you GROUP BY an expression rather than a column name?

options.text: No, only column names are allowed in GROUP BY
Yes — for example: GROUP BY YEAR(OrderDate) groups results by year
Only if the expression is already in a WHERE clause
Only aggregate expressions are allowed

questions.text: What does SUM(DISTINCT Amount) do?

options.text: Sums all Amount values including duplicates
Sums only distinct (unique) Amount values, ignoring duplicates
Returns an error
Counts distinct Amount values

questions.text: In what order are these SQL clauses executed by the database engine? SELECT, FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY

options.text: SELECT → FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → ORDER BY
FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY
WHERE → FROM → GROUP BY → SELECT → HAVING → ORDER BY
FROM → SELECT → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → ORDER BY

questions.text: Which aggregate function would you use to find the earliest order date?

options.text: FIRST(OrderDate)
BOTTOM(OrderDate)
MIN(OrderDate)
EARLIEST(OrderDate)

questions.text: What does GROUP BY ROLLUP(Region, City) produce compared to GROUP BY Region, City?

options.text: The same result
ROLLUP also includes subtotals per Region and a grand total, in addition to the Region-City grouping
ROLLUP only returns the grand total
ROLLUP excludes the Region-City combination

questions.text: Can you use a column alias from SELECT in the HAVING clause?

options.text: Yes, always
No — in standard SQL, HAVING cannot reference SELECT aliases; you must repeat the expression
Only if the alias is numeric
Only in PostgreSQL

questions.text: What does this query return? SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Employees WHERE Department = 'Sales'

options.text: A list of all Sales employees
The number of employees in the Sales department
The sum of salaries in the Sales department
An error because COUNT requires GROUP BY

questions.text: Which query returns the average salary for each job title, only including titles with at least 3 employees?

options.text: SELECT JobTitle, AVG(Salary) FROM Employees WHERE COUNT(*) >= 3 GROUP BY JobTitle
SELECT JobTitle, AVG(Salary) FROM Employees GROUP BY JobTitle HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3
SELECT JobTitle, AVG(Salary) FROM Employees GROUP BY JobTitle WHERE AVG(Salary) > 0 AND COUNT >= 3
SELECT JobTitle, AVG(Salary) FROM Employees HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3

questions.text: If a GROUP BY query produces NULL in an aggregated column from ROLLUP, what does that NULL represent?

options.text: A missing value in the data
A subtotal or grand total row generated by ROLLUP
An error in the grouping
An empty group with no rows

questions.text: Which aggregate function is NOT affected by NULL values (it always includes NULLs in its count)?

options.text: SUM()
AVG()
COUNT(*)
MIN()
